structured.summary: This paper proposes IAPEK, an instance-level adaptive method to selectively augment large pre-trained language models with external knowledge only when necessary, using a novel metric called Thrust to estimate the need for external knowledge. Extensive experiments on a diverse benchmark demonstrate that Thrust improves cost-efficiency and performance in knowledge-enhanced NLP tasks.

strengths.point: Addresses the inefficiency and noise issues in external knowledge retrieval by adaptively deciding when to use external knowledge.
strengths.evidence: Section 1 Introduction and Section 2 Method describe the motivation and design of IAPEK and Thrust to selectively apply external knowledge.

strengths.point: Proposes a novel, lightweight metric (Thrust) based on instance distribution estimation without requiring additional fine-tuning or labels at test time.
strengths.evidence: Section 2.2 details the design of Thrust using clustering of instance representations and distance-based scoring.

strengths.point: Comprehensive experimental evaluation across multiple models, task types (MC classification and open-domain QA), and knowledge sources.
strengths.evidence: Section 3 describes experiments on T5, GPT-J, OPT, UnifiedQA models over 12 datasets with different knowledge formats.

strengths.point: Demonstrates significant improvements in cost-efficiency and performance by selectively applying external knowledge to only a subset of instances.
strengths.evidence: Section 3.4 and 4.1 show that Thrust achieves up to 26% average performance improvement and outperforms full knowledge usage in many cases.

weaknesses.point: The method relies on the assumption that external knowledge is beneficial, which may not hold for all tasks or datasets.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4.1 and 3.4 discuss failure cases such as ARC-C where external knowledge is less useful or noisy.

weaknesses.point: Thrust depends on clustering and representation quality, which may vary across models and tasks, potentially limiting generalizability.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4.3 shows some variation in performance depending on which model layer is used for representations.

weaknesses.point: The paper lacks detailed analysis on the computational overhead of computing Thrust scores and clustering in large-scale or real-time settings.
weaknesses.evidence: While claiming efficiency, the paper does not provide explicit runtime or scalability analysis in Section 3 or 4.

weaknesses.point: Limited discussion on how the method would perform with other types of external knowledge beyond text passages, such as structured knowledge graphs or multimodal data.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 5 Related Work mentions knowledge graphs but experiments focus mainly on text-based knowledge.

weaknesses.point: The threshold selection for deciding which instances receive external knowledge appears heuristic and may require tuning per dataset.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 2.1 mentions setting threshold t as a percentile of scores without detailed ablation or automated selection.

weaknesses.point: The evaluation primarily focuses on accuracy and F1 improvements but does not assess the impact on model calibration or uncertainty estimation.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 3 and 4 report accuracy and F1 but do not analyze confidence calibration or error types.

weaknesses.point: The paper could benefit from clearer presentation and more concise writing, as some sections are dense and occasionally repetitive.
weaknesses.evidence: The full text contains some verbose explanations and formatting issues that may hinder readability.

questions.question: How sensitive is the performance of Thrust to the choice of clustering algorithm and the number of clusters?
questions.motivation: Understanding this would clarify the robustness and practical applicability of the method across different datasets and models.

questions.question: Can Thrust be extended or adapted to handle other forms of external knowledge, such as knowledge graphs or multimodal information?
questions.motivation: This would broaden the impact and applicability of the approach beyond text-based knowledge retrieval.

questions.question: What is the computational overhead of computing Thrust scores and clustering compared to the cost of external knowledge retrieval?
questions.motivation: Quantifying this is important to validate the claimed efficiency gains in real-world scenarios.

questions.question: Have the authors considered integrating Thrust with uncertainty estimation or confidence calibration methods to further improve instance selection?
questions.motivation: Combining these approaches might enhance the reliability of adaptive knowledge augmentation.

critique_points.point_001: The method relies on the assumption that external knowledge is beneficial, which may not hold for all tasks or datasets. (Section 4.1 and 3.4 discuss failure cases such as ARC-C where external knowledge is less useful or noisy.)
critique_points.point_002: Thrust depends on clustering and representation quality, which may vary across models and tasks, potentially limiting generalizability. (Section 4.3 shows some variation in performance depending on which model layer is used for representations.)
critique_points.point_003: The paper lacks detailed analysis on the computational overhead of computing Thrust scores and clustering in large-scale or real-time settings. (While claiming efficiency, the paper does not provide explicit runtime or scalability analysis in Section 3 or 4.)
critique_points.point_004: Limited discussion on how the method would perform with other types of external knowledge beyond text passages, such as structured knowledge graphs or multimodal data. (Section 5 Related Work mentions knowledge graphs but experiments focus mainly on text-based knowledge.)
critique_points.point_005: The threshold selection for deciding which instances receive external knowledge appears heuristic and may require tuning per dataset. (Section 2.1 mentions setting threshold t as a percentile of scores without detailed ablation or automated selection.)
critique_points.point_006: The evaluation primarily focuses on accuracy and F1 improvements but does not assess the impact on model calibration or uncertainty estimation. (Section 3 and 4 report accuracy and F1 but do not analyze confidence calibration or error types.)
critique_points.point_007: The paper could benefit from clearer presentation and more concise writing, as some sections are dense and occasionally repetitive. (The full text contains some verbose explanations and formatting issues that may hinder readability.)
critique_points.point_008: How sensitive is the performance of Thrust to the choice of clustering algorithm and the number of clusters? (Understanding this would clarify the robustness and practical applicability of the method across different datasets and models.)
critique_points.point_009: Can Thrust be extended or adapted to handle other forms of external knowledge, such as knowledge graphs or multimodal information? (This would broaden the impact and applicability of the approach beyond text-based knowledge retrieval.)
critique_points.point_010: What is the computational overhead of computing Thrust scores and clustering compared to the cost of external knowledge retrieval? (Quantifying this is important to validate the claimed efficiency gains in real-world scenarios.)
critique_points.point_011: Have the authors considered integrating Thrust with uncertainty estimation or confidence calibration methods to further improve instance selection? (Combining these approaches might enhance the reliability of adaptive knowledge augmentation.)
